coredata.dc:title: Experimental determination of the constitutive properties of a graphite anode layer in lithium-ion batteries using a bending test method
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Üçel, İ.B.
Gupta, P.
Gudmundson, P.
coredata.dc:description: The stress-strain relationship of a dry lithium-ion graphite anode coating has been characterized by a bending test method. The method is based on U-shaped bending of single-side coated electrodes, which enables separate measurements of tensile and compressive properties of the electrode coating. The experiments reveal that the elastic modulus of the anode coating in compression is higher than the elastic modulus in tension and that the compressive stiffness increases with strain level. Contrary, the tensile modulus is approximately independent of strain. The quantitative results for compressive modulus, and in particular the stiffening effect with increasing strain, are believed to be new to the battery research community. The measured stiffness of the anode coating is compared to previously reported results for a cathode coating. It is found that the anode coating is stiffer in compression compared to the cathode coating despite a much larger particle stiffness of the cathode material in comparison to the anode. It is concluded that differences in porosity are the main reason for the observed behavior. The method also successfully captures the hysteresis effects, both in tension and compression, that are present due to the polymeric binder and the evolution of microstructural contacts. Relaxation experiments are as well conducted to characterize the time-dependent properties of the anode coating, and the response is modeled by a Prony series.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery electrode
U-shape bending test
Stress-strain relationship
Viscoelastic behavior
Hysteresis

Elastic moduli of anode coating calculated at different bending strains with varying displacement rates in (a) compression and (b) tension.
Stress relaxation tests in tension and compression configurations with faceplate separation (Δ) 15 mm kept constant for 6 h and the corresponding Prony series fit.

Relaxation modulus of anode coating based on Prony series fit for 6 h.

head.ce:title: Experimental determination of the constitutive properties of a graphite anode layer in lithium-ion batteries using a bending test method

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Solid Mechanics, Department of Engineering Mechanics, KTH Royal Institute of Technology, Stockholm 100 44, Sweden

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Solid Mechanics, Department of Engineering Mechanics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Solid Mechanics, Department of Engineering Mechanics, KTH Royal Institute of Technology, Stockholm 100 44, Sweden

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The stress-strain relationship of a dry lithium-ion graphite anode coating has been characterized by a bending test method. The method is based on U-shaped bending of single-side coated electrodes, which enables separate measurements of tensile and compressive properties of the electrode coating. The experiments reveal that the elastic modulus of the anode coating in compression is higher than the elastic modulus in tension and that the compressive stiffness increases with strain level. Contrary, the tensile modulus is approximately independent of strain. The quantitative results for compressive modulus, and in particular the stiffening effect with increasing strain, are believed to be new to the battery research community. The measured stiffness of the anode coating is compared to previously reported results for a cathode coating. It is found that the anode coating is stiffer in compression compared to the cathode coating despite a much larger particle stiffness of the cathode material in comparison to the anode. It is concluded that differences in porosity are the main reason for the observed behavior. The method also successfully captures the hysteresis effects, both in tension and compression, that are present due to the polymeric binder and the evolution of microstructural contacts. Relaxation experiments are as well conducted to characterize the time-dependent properties of the anode coating, and the response is modeled by a Prony series.

ce:section.ce:para: As the application of Li-ion batteries becomes more prevalent, the improvement of battery performance is demanded. Battery characteristics such as energy capacity, charging rates, and battery lifetime are determined by battery design, constituent materials of battery components, and operating conditions. Battery electrodes, as elementary components of a battery, consist of conductive metal foils and electrode coatings, which are formed by active particles, polymeric binders, conducting additives, and voids. The mechanical integrity and stability of the electrode coating, which highly influences the battery performance, are degraded by electrochemical and mechanical aging [1–3]. It has been observed that mechanical degradation progresses with electrochemical cycling, which opens up for monitoring the state of a battery [4,5]. The mechanical degradation can appear at multiple length scales, and to understand the limitations of battery performance, it is necessary to develop coupled mechanical-electrochemical models. Reliable mechanical characterization and constitutive behavior are therefore essential for the development of a realistic battery model at any scale. Electrochemical cycling causes electrodes to swell and shrink, which, in turn, generates internal stresses in electrode particles [6–8]. This may potentially result in particle cracking, particle-binder detachment, and void formation at microscopic length scales [9,10]. Consequently, the material properties of the constituents of the electrode coating are required to simulate the material failure at microscopic length scale. The internal stresses arising from electrochemical cycling along with external loadings may as well cause meso and macro-scale failures such as electrode buckling/wrinkling, fracture of the electrode coating, and delamination [11–13]. Thus, it is necessary to characterize the mechanical behavior of electrode coating for mesoscopic and macroscopic level modeling. In the present paper, the elastic and time-dependent constitutive behavior of a dry anode coating is characterized by the U-shape bending method presented in [14].

The particle-binder porous composite structure of an electrode active material imposes a different mechanical response in tension and compression [14]. In previous studies, several types of mechanical experiments have been performed to analyze the characteristics of dry anode and cathode coatings in tension and/or compression [13–20]. Tensile properties of electrode coatings have been determined by uniaxial tensile tests of rectangular electrode specimens. The Young's modulus of the coatings is then obtained by application of the rule of mixtures [15,16,18,19]. However, the uncertainty involved in the extraction of elastic modulus of coating using the tensile tests is very high, since the metal foil of the electrode largely controls the overall tensile behavior of the electrode and the electrode coating has a very small stiffness compared to the metallic current collector.

To measure the compressive properties of the electrode coating with the same method is even more challenging due to the premature buckling of the metal foil as a result of the electrode geometry. Alternative experimental methods have been presented in the literature [13,15,17,18,20]. Uniaxial compression of stacks of electrode layers has been applied to determine the mechanical properties of the electrode coating [13,15,18]. However, indetermined contact conditions between the electrode layers during compression result in uncertainties for the extracted compressive properties of the electrode coating. Furthermore, out-of-plane compressive properties are measured by this method instead of the in-plane properties that generally are of larger importance in applications. Zhu et al. [20] investigated graphite powders of anode and cathode coating materials subjected to powder compaction. Uniaxial compression tests were then applied for the so produced specimens. Even though this method allows the compressive properties of an electrode coating to be individually determined, the porosity is much smaller than that of conventionally manufactured electrode coatings. Furthermore, nano-indentation tests have been utilized to characterize the compressive behavior of the electrode coating [17]. However, the properties obtained by this method are not representative for the whole electrode coating due to the dependency on the application point of the indenter. Also, the compressive strain produced by indentation will be much larger than the strain level anticipated in conventional applications [21].

Aside from the tensile-compressive behavior of an electrode coating, the time-dependent response should also be considered because of the polymeric binder content, which generally shows a viscoelastic behavior [22,23]. As reported in [14], the binder content contributes to the overall mechanical response of the electrode coating, and it can be characterized by a relaxation modulus [24].

The abovementioned methods have limitations, in particular for measurements of compressive properties. Considering the need to improve experimental techniques to capture in-plane compressive and time-dependent responses, the U-shaped bending test method was presented by Gupta et al. [14] to estimate in-plane constitutive behavior for a cathode coating. Single-sided coated electrode specimens subjected to U-shaped bending tests were used to determine tensile and compressive in-plane properties. One of the powerful aspects of this method is the elimination of buckling problems while measuring in-plane compressive properties. In addition, the uncertainties to extract the tensile properties of an electrode coating in a uniaxial tension test are significantly reduced since the contribution to the stiffness is much larger in bending as compared to tension. In Gupta et al. [14], this technique was applied to measure the mechanical properties of a cathode coating. It was demonstrated that highly reliable and reproducible results can be obtained for the stress-strain behavior.

In the present study, the U-shape bending method is used to characterize the in-plane mechanical properties of a graphite coating. The elastic moduli are extracted from the tests using linear elastic beam theory based on small strains and large rotations. To measure the time-dependent behavior, relaxation experiments are as well performed, and the response is modeled by a Prony series. The results for the graphite anode coating are compared to the previously reported results for an NMC cathode coating [14], and the reasons for the differences are discussed.

The lithium-ion battery electrode tested in this paper is a dry commercial graphite anode provided by supplier CUSTOMCELLS®. The specimen is a single-side coated graphite electrode with a layer of thickness 84 µm on a copper foil of thickness 10 µm. The Youngs’ modulus of the used copper foils was measured using tensile tests. Based on the tests, Young´s modulus was estimated as 110 GPa which is in agreement with typical data for copper. The electrode has a specific capacity of 3.9 mAh/cm², and it has been calendared to obtain a porosity of 37%. The electrode layer is composed of graphite particles (95 wt.%) and a polymeric binder (5 wt.%) composed of a mixture of CMC (Carboxymethyl cellulose) and SBR (Styrene Butadiene rubber). Rectangular samples of length 100 mm and width 10 mm are used for mechanical testing, which are cut out from a large electrode sheet. There is a slight variation (± 0.4 mm) in specimen widths after they are cut out. However, this geometric difference does not influence the reliability and the repeatability of the measurements since the measured data are normalized by the specimen width. The samples are carefully cut down using a Rexel Classic Cut™ CL420 cutter such that the edges are not destroyed. Detailed microscopic investigations revealed that the damage introduced by cutting was very limited and it was concluded that this effect could be neglected in the evaluation of stiffnesses. Repeated measurements with different specimen widths were also evaluated, and it was concluded that the damage introduced by cutting is negligible. For all the experimental results presented in this paper, the test specimens used are unique and pristine (subjected to no electrochemical cycling), and the width of each specimen is measured after it is cut out. Fig. 1(a), (b) show the cross-section and top view of the graphite coating taken by Focused ion beam (FIB)/ Scanning electron microscope (SEM). The spatial distribution of the constituents is clearly observed.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the electrode sample subjected to the U-shape bending test with the specimen placed symmetrically between crossheads by gluing the ends of electrodes using tape to the faceplate. The electrode samples are glued to the faceplates so that there is no sliding. Depending on whether the coating is on the outside or inside the bending curvature, the layer is loaded in tension or compression, respectively, see Fig. 2(b), (c). One of the faceplates is kept fixed, while the other faceplate can be moved with varying displacement rates.

For all the tests performed, the initial faceplate separation (Δ) is set as 30 mm. All the mechanical tests presented in this paper were conducted using a ZwickRoell tensile testing machine equipped with a 50 N load cell. The relation between the applied load, P, and the faceplate separation, Δ is given in [14] as
(1)
P = E I
(
1.695
Δ
)
2

The above relation has been derived using linear elastic beam theory, assuming small strains and large rotations [25]. Here, EI refers to the bending stiffness of the whole specimen. Fig. 3 shows the coordinate system used for the analysis and the magnified view of the cross-section of the electrode.

The elastic modulus of the electrode coating is calculated using the slope of P vs.
1 /
Δ
2
curves. Hence, by measuring the force and the faceplate separation, the bending stiffness of the electrode can be obtained.

The bending stiffness, EI, of the whole electrode can be expressed in terms of the elastic modulus
E 1
and thickness
t 1
of the anode coating, the elastic modulus
E 2
and thickness
t 2
of the copper foil as well as the specimen width b by the use of laminate theory. Here, the values of parameters
E 2
,
t 1
,
t 2
and b are known and are given in Section 2.1.

The following equations define the relationship,
(2)
E I =
E 2
{
b
t 2 3
12
+ b
t 2
(
y ¯
−
t 2
2
)
2
+
E 1
E 2
[
b
t 1 3
12
+ b
t 1
(
(
t 1
2
+
t 2
)
−
y ¯
)
2
]
}
where the position of the neutral layer (
y ¯
) is given by,
(3)
y ¯
=
E 1
E 2
(
t 1
2
+
t 2
)
b
t 1
+
t 2
2
b
t 2
E 1
E 2
b
t 1
+ b
t 2

Eqs. (1)–(3) have been used to evaluate the elastic modulus of the anode coating
(
E 1
) from measurements of EI, according to Eq. (1) and known values of
E 2
, b ,
t 1
and
t 2
.

Electrode samples are subjected to load-unload cycles in tension and compression configuration. In order to characterize the elastic modulus of the coating, the stiffness was measured at various unloading steps. In this way, the eventual effects of plasticity on measurements are minimized. At first, the faceplate separation is altered from Δ = 30 mm to Δ = 25 mm and then retracted back 1 mm. This ‘retraction back’ corresponds to the unloading of the specimen. Similarly, unload steps are employed at Δ = 22.5 mm, Δ = 20 mm and Δ = 17.5 mm with 1 mm retraction. Finally, when the faceplate separation becomes 15 mm, the specimen is fully unloaded to the initial faceplate separation (Δ = 30 mm). This is done for both the configurations, tension and compression. A fast displacement rate of 30 mm/min is utilized to minimize the time-dependent effects of the polymeric binder.

The force (P) is plotted vs.
1 /
Δ
2
, see Fig. 4 (a). At each unload step, a linear line is fitted, which gives an estimate of the bending stiffness of the electrode, cf. Eq. (1). It should be noted that at each unload step, the anode coating is subjected to different bending strains. The volume average of bending strain is calculated using linear elastic theory and is presented in Fig. 4(b). With the bending strains in the range of 0.75–1.25% and the displacement rate of 30 mm/min, the elastic modulus is calculated as 1 GPa (average) for tension and in the range of 1.46–3.72 GPa for compression.

As observed in Fig. 4(a), the electrode specimen with the coating subjected to compression shows a significantly higher stiffness compared to tension. In order to portray the mechanical behavior with increasing bending strain, the detailed images of unloading steps are shown in Fig. 4(c), (d). The viscoelastic nature of the binder is reflected by this observed hysteresis [22].

The time-dependent nature of the binder suggests a rate-dependent hysteresis [22], and in order to investigate the effects of rate-dependence on the mechanical behavior of electrode, the experiment is repeated with different displacement rates (0.1, 0.5, 1, 10 mm/min). The corresponding elastic moduli are calculated at each unload step. Fig. 5 shows the calculated elastic modulus at each unload step for the anode coating with different displacement rates. It is noted that the effect of strain rate presented in Fig. 5 is small and that the stiffening effect is slightly larger for smaller strain rates.The strain level in the coating is here represented by the root mean square (RMS) of strain based on a linear elastic analysis, see Fig. 4(b).

Based on the elastic calculations, it is evident from Fig. 5 that there is a significant increase (from 1.28 GPa to 5.05 GPa) in elastic modulus of anode coating with increasing bending strain in compression. However, the variation in elastic modulus in tension is insignificant with increasing bending strain. The observed difference in compression and tension is most likely due to the evolution of particle-particle contacts resulting from the porous microstructure. The presented data for the tensile elastic modulus have been compared to measurements presented in the literature [15,16,18]. The reported tensile elastic modulus of graphite electrodes measured by uniaxial tensile tests are 0.674 GPa in [18], 1.25 GPa in [16], and 2.5 GPa [15]. The differences in comparison to the present data may be due to differences in coating compositions.

The presented compressive stiffnesses have as well been compared to data reported in [17,18]. In Zhang et al. [18], uniaxial compression was applied to a stack of graphite electrodes, and from the stress-strain curve, a compressive modulus of 0.06 GPa can be identified. Even though the stiffening effect with increasing compressive strain is qualitatively matching the trend presented here, there is a large difference in modulus compared to the present study. It is believed that the undefined contact conditions during compression cause the discrepancies. The compressive elastic moduli (1.25–2 GPa) obtained via nano-indentation tests in [17] seem to approximately correlate with the data set in Fig. 5(a). It should, however, be noted that the strain levels at indentation are much larger than the strains considered in the present paper.

To further investigate the time-dependent effects, relaxation experiments are performed. The faceplates are moved from Δ = 30 mm to Δ = 15 mm with the displacement rate of 30 mm/min and are then kept in a constant position for six hours. The force and time are registered using the tensile testing machine. This is performed for both the configurations, tension, and compression. Fig. 6 shows the mechanical response of the electrode under stress relaxation tests.
It is observed that the stresses relax with time. Similar to the previously reported experiments in Fig. 4, the electrode coating is stiffer in compression in comparison to tension.

The stress relaxation is most likely a result of the time-dependent behavior of the binder. To describe the relaxation behavior, a visco-elastic relaxation modulus in the form of a Prony series is applied. The procedure to calculate the Prony series parameters is described in [14]. With the faceplate separation of 15 mm, the relaxation modulus of the electrode coating is presented in Table 1 .

In Fig. 7 , the elastic moduli of the graphite coating are compared to the results for an NMC cathode coating presented in [14]. All specimens have the same 10 × 100 mm2 surface area, and they are tested with 30 mm/min displacement rate. It is noteworthy to mention that the
ε
r m s
of anode and cathode coatings are different due to the different thicknesses of electrode coating of as-received materials. However, the strain levels of the anode (∼0.75%−1.25%) and the cathode (∼1%−1.65%) do not significantly vary from each other to impede the comparison.

The most noticeable similarities between anode and cathode coatings are that they both show the same trend of increasing elastic modulus with increasing compressive strain and that the tensile elastic modulus is approximately independent of strain. This can be attributed to the porous particle structure of coatings regardless of the type of the materials. It is observed that the increase in the compressive elastic modulus with strain is larger for the anode coating compared to the cathode coating. The main differences in morphologies of cathode and anode coatings can be observed in Fig. 8 . The porosity for the anode coating is 37% which can be compared to 51% for the cathode coating.

Because of the smaller binder content in the anode (5 wt.%) compared to the cathode (7 wt.%), the difference in particle volume fraction is even larger. The cathode stiffness is rather well in line with theoretical predictions based on non-percolating particles and thereby binder control of the stiffness [26]. The larger stiffening effect of the anode, despite a smaller particle modulus (32 GPa for graphite [27] versus 200 GPa for NMC [28]) is most likely a result of a higher particle volume fraction in combination with a larger number of particle-particle contacts that increases with compressive strain. The information obtained on the behavior of pristine graphite coating is new, in particular the compressive properties, which can help in developing a robust constitutive model for the whole battery stack.

Furthermore, when the tensile elastic moduli of anode and cathode coatings are compared, a marginally stiffer behavior is observed for the anode coating (Etens= 1 GPa) compared to that of the cathode coating (Etens= 0.61 GPa). The difference in tensile elastic moduli is most likely a result of different binder material content (CMC-SBR for anode, and PVDF for cathode) and the larger particle volume fraction for the anode. This observation is consistent with the results in [29], in which the stress-strain behavior of CMC-SBR and PVDF are compared. A larger tensile elastic modulus for anode coatings compared to cathode coatings has also been observed in [16,18].

In order to minimize mechanical degradation of battery components, it is necessary to consider mechanical stresses that appear with electrochemical cycling. Inside a battery, stresses at various length scales can result from swelling/shrinking of active particles, microstructural changes, external loadings and constraints on battery level, etc. In addition, phenomena like side reactions and gas formation can significantly influence the mechanical degradation of battery constituents. For accurate prediction of the level of stresses, knowledge of the constitutive laws (relations between stresses and deformations) for each of the constituents is required.

The present paper provides useful information concerning the tensile and compressive elastic moduli of an anode coating. The low values of elastic moduli compared to particle stiffness of the anode layer indicate that the mechanical behavior is mainly governed by the porous structure as well as the binder, at least for the strain levels that are reported in the present study. In particular, the quantitative results for stiffening effects in compression are to the authors' knowledge new. The stiffening effect with increasing compressive strains shows that a non-linear constitutive law must be considered for predictions of stresses. Based on the results in the paper, such a model should be formulated and implemented in mechanical simulation software to get reliable results for stress evolution inside the battery.

The experimental results from relaxation tests show that the binder constituents affect the mechanical properties of the electrode coating and that time-dependent effects are present. It is believed that these effects mainly result from the viscoelastic and/or viscoplastic properties of the binder. Apart from a fast initial relaxation, the time dependence is not that large. This indicates that viscoelastic and/or viscoplastic effects can be neglected, at least for intermediate time scales. The stiffness measurements at different strain rates also indicate a weak dependence on strain rate, which is in line with the conclusions from the relaxation experiments.

The present results should also be of interest for the development of micromechanical models on the particle level of electrode coatings. Predicted stiffnesses from the micromechanical models can be compared to measurements and microstructural parameters like binder stiffnesses and contact conditions can be identified. There is a need for more experimental characterization of mechanical properties at both micro and mesoscale. Additionally, to be able to characterize damage evolution and fatigue, it will be important to conduct mechanical measurements concurrently with electrochemical charging and discharging in parallel to the development of micromechanical models. The changes in mechanical properties with electrochemical cycling must be considered to develop a robust constitutive model. The effect of changes in the volume of electrodes with electrochemical must also be considered.

İ.B. Üçel: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. P. Gupta: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. P. Gudmundson: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest. The research has not involved human participants and/or animals.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the Swedish Energy Agency under contracts 45387–1 and 45514–1 is gratefully acknowledged.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Deformation and stress in electrode materials for Li-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Mukhopadhyay, B.W. Sheldon, Deformation and stress in electrode materials for Li-ion batteries, Prog. Mater. Sci.63 (2014) 58–116. 10.1016/j.pmatsci.2014.02.001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ measurements of strains in composite battery electrodes during electrochemical cycling

Soc.162 (2015) A2656–A2663. 10.1149/2.0341514jes.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diffusion induced stress in layered Li-ion battery electrode plates

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modeling of progressive delamination in a thin film driven by diffusion-induced stresses

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Lu, Y. Song, Z. Guo, J. Zhang, Modeling of progressive delamination in a thin film driven by diffusion-induced stresses, Int. J. Solids Struct.50 (2013) 2495–2507. 10.1016/j.ijsolstr.2013.04.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on modeling of electro-chemo-mechanics in lithium-ion batteries

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanical behavior of representative volume elements of lithium-ion battery modules under compressive loading conditions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.J. Lai, M.Y. Ali, J. Pan, Mechanical behavior of representative volume elements of lithium-ion battery modules under compressive loading conditions, J. Power Sources248 (2014) 789–808. 10.1016/j.jpowsour.2013.09.128.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Gupta, I.B. Üçel, P. Gudmundson, E. Olsson, Characterization of the constitutive behavior of a cathode active layer in lithium-ion batteries using a bending test method, Exp. Mech.60 (2020) 847–860. 10.1007/s11340-020-00613-5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microscale failure mechanisms leading to internal short circuit in Li-ion batteries under complex loading scenarios

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Sahraei, E. Bosco, B. Dixon, B. Lai, Microscale failure mechanisms leading to internal short circuit in Li-ion batteries under complex loading scenarios, J. Power Sources319 (2016) 56–65. 10.1016/j.jpowsour.2016.04.005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigation on the blanking properties of thin multi-layer electrode of lithium-ion battery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhu, X. Zhang, H. Luo, E. Sahraei, Investigation of the deformation mechanisms of lithium-ion battery components using in-situ micro tests, Appl. Energy224 (2018) 251–266. 10.1016/j.apenergy.2018.05.007.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zhang, J. Xu, L. Cao, Z. Wu, S. Santhanagopalan, Constitutive behavior and progressive mechanical failure of electrodes in lithium-ion batteries, J. Power Sources357 (2017) 126–137. 10.1016/j.jpowsour.2017.04.103.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.J. Lai, M.Y. Ali, J. Pan, Mechanical behavior of representative volume elements of lithium-ion battery modules under various loading conditions, J. Power Sources248 (2014) 789–808. 10.1016/j.jpowsour.2013.09.128.
